work on c. elegans, is much more exciting, but lacks
public impact... can we do both

--- Cecilia Steen cecilia.steen@gmail.com wrote:

Hi C.. Could you please pass on the email below and
the attached
piece to JEE? This is in response to a magazine
article he fedexed me.

Thanks,
s.

Date: Sat, 25 Feb 2006 16:52:38 -0500
From: "George Church"
g1m1c1@receptor.med.harvard.edu
Subject:
To: smkosslyn@wjh.harvard.edu
Cc: "(George) Church"
g1m1c1@receptor.med.harvard.edu
Reply-to: g1m1c1@receptor.med.harvard.edu
Organization: Harvard
Thread-index: AcXnzHwFN8XPp7lGTsaU4l0z5/EflBShXTJg
Status: O

I am very familiar with Aubrey's work. One of my
current postdocs (Pedro
de
Magahaes) and one of my former students and ex-CEO
of Longenity Inc (Pete
Estep) have worked in that field for a decade each
and I have followed it
longer but less intensely. I have attached a
critique by experts in the
field. Most feel that he is highly active at
getting attention (both good
and bad PR) yet not 'visionary' in any productive
way. He has no
experimental background in a field where deep
biological knowledge almost
certainly matters. But this does raise the
important point that as our
project coalesces, we want to steer clear that sort
of vaguery or hype. If
an intelligent lay overview is desired check the
latest Scientific American
article by two local experts (David Sinclair and
Lenny Guarante). I'd be
happy to update the last RSS draft to incorporate
parts of Wed night's
conversation.

Thanks,
--George

Hi George:

Have you ever heard of a guy at Cambridge
University named Aubrey de Grey?
Jeffrey just fedexed me an interview with him
(which appeared in a magazine
called "Life Extension." I can't tell if he's a
crank or a visionary (or
both).

If you are interested, I'll send a copy over to
you..

Thanks,
s.

--

//////////////////////////////////////_/

--- Cecilia Steen <cecilia.steen@gmail.com> wrote:

> Hi Cecilia:
> 
> Could you please pass the below and attached to JEE?
> 
> Thanks,
> s.
> 
> ---------------------
> 
> Jeffrey..
> 
> So: Here is a list of invitees, possible dates

--- Cecilia Steen cecilia.steen@gmail.com wrote:

Hi Cecilia:

Could you please pass the below and attached to JEE?

Thanks,
s.

Jeffrey..

So: Here is a list of invitees, possible dates
(please eliminate the ones
that don't work for you), and a brief stab at a note
to be included with the
invitation (change as you see fit). I've also
attached something George put
together, outlining the Rebuilding the Ship at Sea
(RSS) Project.

Onward, upward!

Thanks,
s.

I. Invitees for dinner

  1. Don Rubin (Statistics; biostatistics and novel
    analyses)
  2. David Laibson (Economics; neuroimaging of
    temporal discounting)
  3. Andrei Shleifer (Economics; roles of attitudes
    and beliefs in political
    persuasion)
  4. Martin Nowak (Mathematics; game theory)
  5. Gary Ruvkun (NASA Mars project, aging,
    development, microbial
    phylogeny).
  6. Joe Jacobson (MediaLab, E-ink, nanofab, synthetic
    genomics)
  7. Stephen M. Kosslyn (psychology, neuroimaging,
    task design)
  8. George Church (computational biology, genomics,
    bioenergy, synth.cells)
  9. George Daley (therapeutic stem cells, cancer)
  10. Sue Dymecki (programming DNA recombination and
    neural lineages)
  11. Xandra Breakfield (gene delivery to neuronal
    cells, MRI/NIR/Biolum)
  12. Cliff Tabin (limb regeneration, handedness)
  13. Richard Mulligan (gene therapy - blood cells)

II. Possible dates for Stephen and George:

February: 16, 21, 22, 23
March: 6, 8. 9, 13, 16, 20, 21. 22, 23

III. Mission/invitation statement

The MediaLab at MIT has turned out to be
intellectually deep and great fun.
We would like to explore a similar idea, but
centered on genetics and the
brain. This XLab would push the envelope, exploring
far-out ideas such as
life extension and enhanced learning. We write to
invite you to a dinner to
explore this general idea.
